section.title: The Reign of Amaziah
section.sectionRationale: Amaziah does what is right, but not wholeheartedly. He obeys a prophet's word to dismiss hired Israelite troops before defeating Edom, then inexplicably adopts the very gods of the people he has just conquered. A second prophet's rebuke is silenced with threats, pride over his victory provokes a reckless challenge to Israel's king, and Judah is routed, Jerusalem's wall broken down, and Amaziah eventually assassinated in a conspiracy.
section.thesis: Amaziah's reign, right but never wholehearted, shows that partial obedience still leaves room for pride and idolatry to take root, so that a real victory won by trusting God's word can be followed almost immediately by a real defeat brought on by trusting one's own success.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Amaziah's justice tempered with incomplete devotion, verses 1 to 4; his obedience in dismissing Israel's troops and his victory over Edom, verses 5 to 13; his sudden turn to Edom's gods and the prophet's rebuke, verses 14 to 16; his prideful challenge to Israel and Judah's defeat, verses 17 to 24; and his death by conspiracy, verses 25 to 28.

verses.text: Amaziah was twenty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem twenty-nine years. His mother's name was Jehoaddan; she was from Jerusalem.
And he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, but not {{shalem|wholeheartedly}}.
As soon as the kingdom was firmly in his grasp, Amaziah executed the servants who had murdered his father the king.

Yet he did not put their sons to death, but acted according to what is written in the Law, in the Book of Moses, where the LORD commanded: "Fathers must not be put to death for their children, and children must not be put to death for their fathers; each is to die for his own sin."

units.label: Amaziah's victories
units.note: the LORD is able to give you much more

verses.text: Then Amaziah gathered the people of Judah and assigned them according to their families to commanders of thousands and of hundreds. And he numbered those twenty years of age or older throughout Judah and Benjamin and found 300,000 chosen men able to serve in the army, bearing the spear and shield.
He also hired 100,000 mighty men of valor from Israel for a hundred talents of silver.
But a man of God came to him and said, "O king, do not let the army of Israel go with you, for the LORD is not with Israel, not with any of the Ephraimites.
Even if you go and fight bravely in battle, God will overthrow you before the enemy, for God has power to help and power to overthrow."
Amaziah asked the man of God, "What should I do about the hundred talents I have given to the troops of Israel?" And the man of God replied, "The LORD is able to give you much more than this."
So Amaziah dismissed the troops who had come to him from Ephraim and sent them home. And they were furious with Judah and returned home in great anger.
Amaziah, however, summoned his strength and led his troops to the Valley of Salt, where he struck down 10,000 men of Seir,
and the army of Judah also captured 10,000 men alive. They took them to the top of a cliff and threw them down so that all were dashed to pieces.
Meanwhile the troops that Amaziah had dismissed from battle raided the cities of Judah, from Samaria to Beth-horon. They struck down 3,000 people and carried off a great deal of plunder.

units.label: Amaziah rebuked for idolatry
units.note: why have you sought this people's gods

verses.text: When Amaziah returned from the slaughter of the Edomites, he brought back the gods of the Seirites, set them up as his own gods, bowed before them, and burned sacrifices to them.
Therefore the anger of the LORD burned against Amaziah, and He sent him a prophet, who said, "Why have you {{darash|sought}} this people's gods, which could not deliver them from your hand?"

While he was still speaking, the king asked, "Have we made you the counselor to the king? Stop! Why be struck down?" So the prophet stopped, but he said, "I know that God has determined to destroy you, because you have done this and have not heeded my advice."
Then Amaziah king of Judah took counsel and sent word to the king of Israel Jehoash son of Jehoahaz, the son of Jehu. "Come, let us meet face to face," he said.

But Jehoash king of Israel replied to Amaziah king of Judah: "A thistle in Lebanon sent a message to a cedar in Lebanon, saying, 'Give your daughter to my son in marriage.' Then a wild beast in Lebanon came along and trampled the thistle.
You have said, 'Look, I have defeated Edom,' and your heart has become {{rum|proud}} and boastful. Now stay at home. Why should you stir up trouble so that you fall, you and Judah with you?"
But Amaziah would not listen, for this had come from God in order to deliver them into the hand of Jehoash, because they had sought the gods of Edom.
So Jehoash king of Israel advanced, and he and Amaziah king of Judah faced each other at Beth-shemesh in Judah.
And Judah was routed before Israel, and every man fled to his own home.

There at Beth-shemesh, Jehoash king of Israel captured Amaziah king of Judah, the son of Joash, the son of Jehoahaz. Then Jehoash brought him to Jerusalem and broke down the wall of Jerusalem from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate, a section of four hundred cubits.
He took all the gold and silver and all the articles found in the house of God with Obed-edom and in the treasuries of the royal palace, as well as some hostages. Then he returned to Samaria.

units.label: The death of Amaziah
units.note: a conspiracy was formed against him

verses.text: Amaziah son of Joash king of Judah lived for fifteen years after the death of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel.
As for the rest of the acts of Amaziah, from beginning to end, are they not written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel?
From the time that Amaziah turned from following the LORD, a conspiracy was formed against him in Jerusalem, and he fled to Lachish. But men were sent after him to Lachish, and they killed him there.
They carried him back on horses and buried him with his fathers in the City of Judah.

shalem.gloss: Whole, complete, or at peace. Amaziah did right, but not shalem, not wholeheartedly, the same root behind shalom; his obedience was real but divided, lacking the complete devotion the LORD desires and that later characterizes the fullest commendations of Judah's kings.

darash.gloss: To seek, inquire, or resort to. The prophet's rebuke turns the same word used elsewhere for seeking the LORD into an indictment: why have you darash, sought, this people's gods, exposing the absurdity of seeking help from gods that could not even save their own worshipers from Amaziah's hand.

rum.gloss: To be high, lifted up, or exalted. Jehoash tells Amaziah his heart has become rum, lifted up, proud, over his victory over Edom; the same root describes pride's characteristic movement, an inflated heart that precedes a very literal fall.

basic.context: Amaziah begins well, executing justice according to God's law rather than the surrounding culture's practice of punishing an entire family for one man's crime. He obeys a prophet's hard word to dismiss a hired army from Israel before a costly war, trusting that the LORD can give him far more than the silver he loses, and wins a real victory over Edom. But immediately afterward he takes the very gods of the people he has just defeated and worships them himself, provoking a prophet's rebuke that he silences with threats. Puffed up by his victory, he recklessly challenges Israel's king to battle, is soundly defeated, watches Jerusalem's wall torn down and the temple plundered, and years later dies in a conspiracy after fleeing to Lachish.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a king whose obedience was genuine but never complete. The chronicler's own summary, right, but not wholeheartedly, frames the whole chapter: it is not that Amaziah did nothing right, but that his partial devotion left room for both extraordinary obedience and shocking idolatry to exist side by side in the same reign.
basic.originalLanguages: Amaziah's obedience is right but not shalem, whole or complete, a telling contrast to the fuller devotion later kings like Hezekiah and Josiah display. The prophet's rebuke plays on darash, seek, turning the language of seeking the LORD into an indictment of seeking dead idols instead. Jehoash's warning names the specific danger: Amaziah's heart has become rum, lifted up, proud, over one victory, setting up his fall.
basic.typology: Amaziah's dismissal of hired mercenaries in obedience to a prophetic word, trusting God to supply far more than the silver he lost, previews the pattern of counting all things loss for the sake of gaining Christ, while his subsequent worship of the defeated gods of Edom is a sober warning against turning any victory, even one God has given, into an occasion for greater unfaithfulness.
basic.christ: Amaziah's willingness to lose a hundred talents of silver in obedience to God's word, trusting that the LORD is able to give much more than this, models the kind of costly trust the gospel calls for, counting present loss as nothing against what God supplies. Yet Amaziah's own subsequent unfaithfulness, worshiping the gods of the very enemy God had just defeated for him, shows the deep need for a king whose heart is truly whole toward God, not merely right in isolated moments, a wholeness found only in Christ.
basic.god: God is faithful to His word, giving Amaziah victory when he trusts and obeys, even at real cost. But God is also just and will not be mocked; He hands the proud king over to defeat precisely because Amaziah sought the gods that could not save Edom, and He brings a final reckoning even after years have passed, showing that turning from the LORD, however delayed the consequence, is never without cost.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 24:16 gives the law of individual responsibility that Amaziah rightly follows in verse 4. Proverbs 16:18 warns that pride goes before destruction, exactly the pattern of Amaziah's fall. Philippians 3:8 speaks of counting all things loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, echoing Amaziah's initial willingness to lose silver for obedience. James 4:6 states that God opposes the proud, fulfilled in Amaziah's humiliating defeat.
basic.oneStory: Amaziah's reign, right but not wholehearted, shows how partial obedience can produce real victories and yet leave a heart vulnerable to pride and even absurd idolatry in the same breath. His story warns every reader that a single act of costly trust does not guarantee an undivided heart, and it points forward to the need for a king whose devotion to God is not partial but complete, the whole hearted righteousness found only in Christ.

context.literary: The chapter's structure deliberately juxtaposes obedience and folly: right action toward the murderers' sons, obedient dismissal of Israel's troops, victory over Edom, then almost immediately, worship of Edom's own defeated gods, prophetic rebuke silenced, prideful challenge, and catastrophic defeat, a whiplash pattern that embodies the summary judgment of verse 2.
context.historical: The hiring of mercenary troops from the northern kingdom for a hundred talents of silver reflects the still uneasy but occasionally cooperative relations between the divided kingdoms; the wall broken down from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate represents a substantial section of Jerusalem's defenses, a humiliating and strategically significant loss.
context.canonical: Amaziah's summary, right, but not wholeheartedly, stands in the chronicler's larger pattern of evaluating each king's heart, anticipating the fuller contrast to come with Hezekiah and especially Josiah, of whom it will be said that no king before or after him turned to the LORD with all his heart, soul, and strength.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Amaziah's obedience, though real, as seen in his just treatment of the assassins' sons and his costly dismissal of Israel's mercenaries, was undermined by a heart not wholly given to the LORD, so that pride and even absurd idolatry toward defeated gods could rise up in the very same reign.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific king's mixed record; it is not suggesting that any single act of obedience or disobedience defines a whole life. What endures is the pattern that partial devotion to God leaves room for both real faithfulness and real folly, and the call to pursue a whole, not divided, heart.

debates.question: Why would Amaziah, having just won by trusting the LORD's word through a prophet, immediately turn to worship the gods of the very people he defeated?

views.label: Confused reasoning that success itself came from these gods
views.case: Some understand Amaziah's act as a superstitious, if illogical, assumption that adopting the religious practices of a defeated but formerly successful people might add to his own power, a common ancient near eastern impulse to appropriate the perceived strengths of conquered nations.

views.label: A heart never fully surrendered even amid genuine obedience
views.case: The chronicler's own framing, right, but not wholeheartedly, verse 2, suggests this act simply reveals what was true all along: obedience to a prophet's word about mercenaries did not equal a heart wholly devoted to the LORD alone, leaving room for this otherwise inexplicable idolatry.

debates.bearing: Both readings underline the same warning: a heart not fully given to God can obey impressively in one moment and act with shocking folly in the next, since partial devotion does not guard against the deeper pull toward other gods.

study.originalLanguages: The chronicler's verdict on Amaziah, right, but not shalem, whole or complete, frames his entire reign. The prophet's rebuke ironically applies darash, seek, the same verb elsewhere used of seeking the LORD, to Amaziah's absurd seeking of defeated gods. Jehoash's warning names the danger precisely: Amaziah's heart has become rum, lifted up in pride, the very condition Scripture consistently says precedes a fall.

typology.type: Costly obedience that trusts God to supply far more than what is given up
typology.fulfillment: The gospel call to count all things loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, Philippians 3:7-8.

typology.type: A king whose devotion is real but divided, right without being wholehearted
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true King whose obedience to the Father was perfect and undivided, Hebrews 5:8-9.

study.christ: Amaziah's obedience in dismissing a hundred talents worth of hired troops, trusting the prophet's word that the LORD is able to give you much more than this, models the kind of costly, forward looking trust the gospel calls for, counting present loss as nothing against what God supplies, Philippians 3:8. Yet his very next act, worshiping the defeated gods of Edom, exposes the limits of obedience that is right without being wholehearted, pointing to the need for a greater King whose devotion to the Father never wavered, wholly obedient even to death on a cross, Philippians 2:8.
study.god: God rewards genuine trust even when it is costly, proving true to His word that He can give far more than what obedience requires giving up. Yet God is not mocked by divided devotion; He is provoked to real anger by absurd idolatry, silences no true rebuke forever, and brings a proud king low precisely at the point of his greatest apparent success, showing that pride, wherever it takes root, invites its own humbling.

crossReferences.note: The law of individual responsibility that Amaziah rightly follows in sparing the sons of his father's murderers.
Pride goes before destruction, a haughty spirit before a fall, exactly the pattern of Amaziah's boastful challenge and subsequent defeat.
Paul's willingness to count all things as loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, echoing Amaziah's initial costly obedience.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, fulfilled in Amaziah's humiliation before Jehoash.

study.oneStory: Amaziah's reign, right but not wholehearted, shows how a real act of costly obedience can stand beside an almost inexplicable act of idolatry within the very same heart, and how pride over one victory can set up a far greater fall. His story calls every reader beyond partial, situational obedience to the whole hearted devotion that only Christ, the truly obedient King, both models perfectly and makes possible in those united to Him.

quiz.q: How does the chronicler summarize Amaziah's obedience to the LORD?

options.text: He did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, but not wholeheartedly.
options.note: Verse 2 gives this exact, carefully qualified summary that frames the whole chapter.

options.text: He did everything exactly as his father Joash had commanded him.
options.note: The summary concerns Amaziah's own relationship to the LORD, not simply following his father's example, and is explicitly qualified as incomplete.

options.text: He completely rejected the LORD from the start of his reign.
options.note: The text states he did what was right, even if not wholeheartedly, not that he rejected the LORD outright.

options.text: No evaluation of Amaziah's faithfulness is given.
options.note: Verse 2 gives an explicit, carefully worded evaluation of his obedience.

quiz.q: Why did Amaziah dismiss the hundred thousand troops he had hired from Israel?

options.text: A man of God warned him that the LORD was not with Israel, and that fighting with them would lead to defeat regardless of bravery.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 record this exact prophetic warning that led to the dismissal.

options.text: The troops refused to fight and deserted on their own.
options.note: The text shows Amaziah actively dismissed them following prophetic counsel; they did not desert independently.

options.text: Judah's own army was already large enough without them.
options.note: The stated reason is the prophetic warning about God's absence from Israel's troops, not merely sufficient numbers in Judah's own army.

options.text: No reason is given for their dismissal.
options.note: Verses 7 to 9 give an explicit, detailed reason for the dismissal.

quiz.q: What did Amaziah do immediately after his victory over Edom that provoked God's anger?

options.text: He brought back the gods of the Seirites, set them up as his own gods, and worshiped and sacrificed to them.
options.note: Verse 14 records this act of idolatry, which directly provoked the LORD's anger in verse 15.

options.text: He built a grand new temple to the LORD in Jerusalem.
options.note: The text records the opposite, worship of defeated foreign gods, not a temple built to the LORD.

options.text: He returned all the plunder to the defeated Edomites.
options.note: The text describes Amaziah keeping and worshiping the idols of the defeated people, not returning plunder to them.

options.text: No idolatry is recorded following his victory.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly records this specific act of idolatry.

quiz.q: What warning did Jehoash king of Israel give Amaziah before their battle?

options.text: Through a parable about a thistle trampled by a wild beast, he warned Amaziah that pride over his victory would lead to his own downfall.
options.note: Verses 18 to 19 record this parable and its explicit warning about Amaziah's pride.

options.text: He promised to surrender to Amaziah without a fight.
options.note: Jehoash instead warned Amaziah against provoking a battle he would lose, not promising surrender.

options.text: He offered to form a permanent alliance with Judah.
options.note: The message is a warning against pride and conflict, not a proposal for alliance.

options.text: No warning was given before the battle.
options.note: Verses 18 to 19 record a detailed, explicit warning through parable.

quiz.q: How did Amaziah's life ultimately end?

options.text: A conspiracy formed against him in Jerusalem after he turned from the LORD, and he was killed after fleeing to Lachish.
options.note: Verse 27 records this conspiracy and his death at Lachish, explicitly tied to his turning from the LORD.

options.text: He died peacefully in Jerusalem, honored by all the people.
options.note: The text records a violent death by conspiracy after fleeing the capital, not a peaceful, honored death.

options.text: He was killed in battle against Israel at Beth-shemesh.
options.note: He was captured, not killed, at Beth-shemesh; his death came later, years afterward, through a separate conspiracy.

options.text: The text does not record how his reign ended.
options.note: Verses 27 to 28 give a specific account of the conspiracy and his death.

questions: Amaziah did what was right, but not wholeheartedly. Where in your own life is your obedience to God real but divided, right in form but not fully given?

Amaziah obeyed a hard, costly word from a prophet, dismissing troops he had already paid for. What costly obedience is God currently asking of you, and what is stopping you from trusting Him for more than you would lose?

Almost immediately after a real victory, Amaziah turned to worship the gods of the very people God had just defeated for him. What has a recent success tempted you to trust or pursue instead of the God who gave it?
Amaziah silenced the second prophet's warning with a threat rather than repentance. How do you typically respond when someone points out a fault in you, defensiveness or genuine self examination?
Pride over one victory led Amaziah into a battle that ended in humiliating defeat. Where might a recent success be inflating your confidence in a way that could set you up for a fall?

translationNotes.note: The quotation follows Deuteronomy 24:16 nearly verbatim, showing Amaziah's genuine, deliberate obedience to written Torah rather than the common ancient near eastern practice of executing an entire family for one member's crime.
A hundred talents of silver is approximately 3.77 tons or 3.42 metric tons, an enormous sum for Amaziah to forfeit in obedience to the prophet's warning.
Four hundred cubits is approximately 600 feet or 182.9 meters of Jerusalem's wall, a substantial and strategically significant breach left by Jehoash's victory.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 25:1-28. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
